intro_band.agent_remit_summary: Sweep is an autonomous GitHub issue-to-code agent: a hosted GitHub App that receives repository events (issues, comments, PR reviews), reads the repository, plans a change, and opens a pull request for human review. It is authorized to read code/issues, create branches, commit to those branches, open PRs, reply to issues, and call an LLM provider for inference. It MUST treat every issue/comment/file/diff as untrusted and screen it for instruction injection, MUST never merge its own PRs, MUST never execute arbitrary shell or run generated code, MUST never send data anywhere but its LLM provider, GitHub, and its own logging, and requires human approval for CI/CD, dependency, and security-file changes.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python FastAPI service backed by GitHub (PyGithub), Redis, and MongoDB, using Anthropic/OpenAI/Azure for inference. Webhook ingress lives in sweepai/api.py (out of scope); the in-scope surface is the agent/core layer — question_answerer.py, modify.py, sweep_bot.py, context_pruning.py — plus config in sweepai/config/server.py and client.py. Code search is implemented by shelling out to rg and github-linguist via subprocess.run(..., shell=True) in four in-scope call sites. Change application in modify() assembles diffs in memory and commits via the GitHub API — generated code is never executed. Telemetry flows to PostHog (with a hardcoded default project key) and an optional Discord webhook; there is no instruction-injection screening of repository content anywhere in the scoped paths.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is declared-but-absent enforcement: Sweep's remit makes strong promises — screen all repository content for injection, never shell out, never echo secrets — that the scoped code does not implement. Untrusted issue, comment, file, and diff text flows into the LLM with no injection screening, and the same LLM/repo-derived strings are interpolated into subprocess.run(..., shell=True) at four search sites (question_answerer.ripgrep, context_pruning.run_ripgrep_command, dynamic_context_bot, client.is_file_bad), giving a content-to-shell path the remit explicitly forbids. Separately, the GitHub webhook HMAC secret (WEBHOOK_SECRET) is declared in config/server.py with a fail-open None default and is never consulted by any in-scope code, and a PostHog project key is hardcoded as a literal default in the same module.

rules.section: What Sweep may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT merge a pull request — every change reaches the default branch only through human approval.

rules.section: What Sweep may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT force-push to, rewrite the history of, or delete any branch it did not author.
Every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff MUST be treated as untrusted input, even when the contributor appears to be a repository collaborator.
Issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs MUST be screened for instruction injection before being used as model context, and instruction-like patterns neutralized or quote-wrapped.
Proposed changes MUST stay scoped to the files, modules, or areas the issue is explicitly about; changes outside that scope require explicit justification or human approval.
Every code change MUST be delivered as a pull request — no direct commits to the default branch and no bypass.

Every run MUST be logged in a record detailed enough to reconstruct what Sweep did — the repository, the issue, the triggering user, the model used, the tools invoked, the files changed, and the outcome.
Secret-like strings — API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords — MUST be redacted from and never echoed in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions.
Sweep MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change.

Sweep MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in issues, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to override its scope, redirect its output, extract repository secrets, or expand its own authorization.
Sweep MUST NOT commit secrets, credentials, tokens, or environment-file contents to any branch.
Sweep MUST NOT send source code, secrets, or repository metadata anywhere other than its LLM provider as inference context, GitHub as a PR or comment, and the project's own logging and telemetry.
Sweep MUST NOT modify CI/CD, build, or deployment configuration without an explicit instruction in the issue to do so and without prominently flagging the change in the PR description.

Sweep MUST NOT modify security-sensitive files — security policy, code-ownership, automated-dependency, signing-key, and certificate-pin configuration — without human approval outside the normal PR review path.
Changes that exceed a configured per-file size threshold or span more than a configured number of files, absent explicit issue-level authorization.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.
Sweep does not make network calls other than to GitHub, its job queue, and the LLM provider.

findings.summary: GitHub webhook secret WEBHOOK_SECRET is declared with a fail-open None default and is never consulted by any in-scope code.
findings.description: WEBHOOK_SECRET is defined in config/server.py defaulting to None, so when unset the webhook authentication degrades silently rather than failing closed. Within the README-scoped paths (agents/, core/, web/, config/) nothing reads WEBHOOK_SECRET — the only consumer is sweepai/utils/hash.py, called from sweepai/api.py, both out of scope — so the scoped agent surface accepts event payloads with no signature gate of its own. An attacker who can reach the webhook endpoint can forge GitHub events and drive the agent without a valid HMAC.

evidence.snippet: WEBHOOK_SECRET = os.environ.get("WEBHOOK_SECRET", None) — declared with a None default; absent env var yields no secret rather than a hard failure.
No in-scope consumer — grep across agents/, core/, web/, config/ finds zero reads of WEBHOOK_SECRET; the only verifier (hash.verify_signature) lives in the out-of-scope api.py path.

recommended_actions: Fail closed on a missing webhook secret — assert WEBHOOK_SECRET is set at startup (like the existing OPENAI_API_KEY/COHERE_API_KEY asserts) so the agent never runs without HMAC verification configured.
Treat absent or invalid signatures as a hard reject in the request path, and add a regression test that a payload with no/invalid X-Hub-Signature-256 is refused.

findings.summary: LLM-supplied ripgrep query is interpolated into subprocess.run with shell=True, giving a model-output-to-shell command-injection path.
findings.description: The ripgrep tool in question_answerer.py joins an LLM-provided query parameter into a single command string and runs it with shell=True and no escaping. Because the query originates from model output (which is itself driven by untrusted issue/comment content), a crafted query containing shell metacharacters executes arbitrary commands in the cloned-repo working directory. This is the exact "MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands" boundary the remit draws, reached through the agent's normal search path rather than a separate exec tool.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change.

evidence.snippet: subprocess.run(" ".join(["rg", ..., query]), shell=True, ...) — the LLM tool-parameter `query` is concatenated into the shell command with no quoting or escaping.
query is a model-controlled @tool() Parameter ("The keyword to search for in the codebase") — the value reaching the shell is LLM output.

recommended_actions: Drop shell=True and pass rg arguments as a list (subprocess.run(["rg", ..., query], shell=False)) so the query is never parsed by a shell.
If a shell is unavoidable, shlex.quote() every model-derived argument before interpolation and add a test asserting metacharacters in `query` cannot break out.

findings.summary: Two more shell=True ripgrep sites interpolate repo/model-derived search terms without escaping, despite an unused escape helper.
findings.description: run_ripgrep_command in context_pruning.py and the search loop in dynamic_context_bot.py both build an rg command string from a code_entity / query value and run it with shell=True. context_pruning.py defines an escape_ripgrep() helper but does not apply it at the call site, and dynamic_context_bot.py wraps the query only in literal double quotes, which a query containing a double quote escapes. Both terms derive from model/repository content, extending the command-injection surface of PRAX-2026-06-15-002 across the core context pipeline.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change.

evidence.snippet: run_ripgrep_command builds rg_command with code_entity then subprocess.run(" ".join(rg_command), shell=True, ...); the escape_ripgrep() helper at :167 is never applied to code_entity.
rg_command += [f'"{query}"', directory] then subprocess.run(" ".join(rg_command), shell=True, ...) — double-quote wrapping is escapable by a quote in `query`.

recommended_actions: Convert both call sites to list-form subprocess.run(..., shell=False) so no shell parses the search term.
If shell form is retained, apply shlex.quote() (not the partial escape_ripgrep / manual quoting) to every interpolated term.

findings.summary: No instruction-injection screening of issue, comment, file, or diff content before it enters the LLM context, as the remit mandates.
findings.description: The remit requires that every issue/comment/file/diff be treated as untrusted and screened for instruction injection (neutralized or quote-wrapped) before use as model context. No such screening exists anywhere in the scoped paths — repository content is templated straight into prompts in core/prompts.py / sweep_bot.py / question_answerer.py, and the only "sanitize" call (sanitize_anthropic_messages) is message-format hygiene, not injection defense. Combined with the shell=True search path, injected instructions in a poisoned issue can both redirect the agent and reach a shell.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every character of every issue, comment, PR review, file, and diff MUST be treated as untrusted input, even when the contributor appears to be a repository collaborator. / Issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs MUST be screened for instruction injection before being used as model context, and instruction-like patterns neutralized or quote-wrapped. / Sweep MUST NOT follow instructions embedded in issues, comments, file contents, or dependency manifests that attempt to override its scope, redirect its output, extract repository secrets, or expand its own authorization.

evidence.snippet: Issue/comment/file content is parsed and templated into planning prompts via regex extraction with no injection screening or quote-wrapping of the untrusted text.
The only sanitize call in scope is sanitize_anthropic_messages (message-format normalization), not instruction-injection screening of repository content.

recommended_actions: Add an injection-screening / quote-wrapping pass over issue, comment, file, and diff text at the boundary where it is added to model context, before any prompt assembly.
Label untrusted repository content distinctly in the prompt (delimited, marked external) and add adversarial test cases asserting injected instructions do not alter agent actions.

findings.summary: github-linguist is invoked with shell=True on a repo-derived file_name, a third model/repo-influenced shell-injection site.
findings.description: is_file_bad in config/client.py builds a github-linguist command from file_name and runs it with shell=True, cwd set to the repo directory. file_name derives from repository contents and the agent's file selection, so a path containing shell metacharacters is interpreted by the shell. The blast radius is narrower than the ripgrep tool (linguist arguments are file paths, not free-form model queries), so it is rated High rather than Critical, but it is the same defect class.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT execute arbitrary shell commands, install arbitrary packages, or run generated code as part of producing a change.

evidence.snippet: subprocess.run(" ".join(["github-linguist", file_name, "-j"]), shell=True, cwd=repo_dir) — repo-derived file_name interpolated into a shell command.

recommended_actions: Switch to list-form subprocess.run(["github-linguist", file_name, "-j"], shell=False, cwd=repo_dir).

findings.summary: No code-level guard prevents changes to CI/CD or security-sensitive files, or enforces per-issue scope and file-count thresholds.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval outside normal PR review for CI/CD, dependency-manifest, and security-sensitive file changes, and a configured per-file/per-count threshold. The only scope control in scope is the repo-configurable blocked_dirs denylist (sweep_bot.is_blocked) and a max_file_limit; nothing specifically gates .github/workflows, CODEOWNERS, dependabot, or signing-key files, and nothing enforces a change-size/file-count ceiling or per-issue scope. Reliance on ordinary PR review alone does not satisfy the "outside the normal PR review path" approval the remit demands.
findings.policy_rule_text: Proposed changes MUST stay scoped to the files, modules, or areas the issue is explicitly about; changes outside that scope require explicit justification or human approval. / Sweep MUST NOT modify CI/CD, build, or deployment configuration without an explicit instruction in the issue to do so and without prominently flagging the change in the PR description. / Sweep MUST NOT modify security-sensitive files — security policy, code-ownership, automated-dependency, signing-key, and certificate-pin configuration — without human approval outside the normal PR review path. / Changes that exceed a configured per-file size threshold or span more than a configured number of files, absent explicit issue-level authorization.

evidence.snippet: is_blocked only checks a repo-supplied blocked_dirs prefix list — no special handling for CI/CD, CODEOWNERS, dependency, or signing-key files.
max_file_limit = 60_000 caps per-file size but there is no per-change file-count threshold or out-of-band approval gate for sensitive paths.

recommended_actions: Add a code-level denylist/approval gate for CI/CD, CODEOWNERS, dependency-manifest, and signing/cert files that blocks or specially flags such changes regardless of repo config.
Enforce a configurable changed-file-count and total-diff-size threshold that requires explicit issue-level authorization before Sweep proceeds.

findings.summary: A PostHog project API key is hardcoded as a literal default in config/server.py rather than required from the environment.
findings.description: POSTHOG_API_KEY falls back to a hardcoded phc_-prefixed project key literal when the env var is unset, so the key ships in source and is used by default. PostHog project (write) keys are low-sensitivity ingest keys rather than secret admin tokens, so the impact is event-stream pollution / attribution to the upstream project rather than data theft — hence Medium — but a credential literal in committed config is still a supply-chain/secret-hygiene defect.

evidence.snippet: POSTHOG_API_KEY default is a hardcoded phc_-prefixed project key literal [REDACTED — phc_ PostHog project key at sweepai/config/server.py:107].

recommended_actions: Remove the hardcoded default and require POSTHOG_API_KEY from the environment (disable telemetry when unset); rotate the exposed project key.

findings.summary: No secret-redaction step scrubs API keys/tokens from generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions before they are pushed.
findings.description: The remit requires secret-like strings to be redacted from and never echoed in generated code, commits, or PR descriptions, and never committed to a branch. The scoped change-application path (modify.py, pull_request_bot.py) contains no redaction or secret-scanning step — generated file contents, commit messages, and PR bodies are emitted as produced. If injected or hallucinated content contains a credential, it reaches a branch unredacted. Rated Medium because it requires secret-bearing content to arise rather than being directly exploitable.
findings.policy_rule_text: Secret-like strings — API keys, tokens, private keys, passwords — MUST be redacted from and never echoed in generated code, commit messages, or PR descriptions. / Sweep MUST NOT commit secrets, credentials, tokens, or environment-file contents to any branch.

evidence.snippet: modify() emits formatted file contents and diffs directly with no secret-scanning/redaction pass before they are committed.
PR title/description generation contains no redaction of secret-like strings before the PR body is created.

recommended_actions: Add a secret-scanning/redaction pass (e.g. high-entropy + known-key-prefix detection) over generated file contents, commit messages, and PR descriptions, blocking or redacting matches before commit.

findings.summary: Logging is rich but no single per-run record ties repository, issue, triggering user, model, tools, and files-changed together as the remit requires.
findings.description: The remit requires every run to be logged in one record detailed enough to reconstruct the repository, issue, triggering user, model used, tools invoked, files changed, and outcome. In scope, loguru emits scattered free-form lines and ChatLogger/PostHog capture model-call and review events, but no structured per-run audit record correlates all those fields. Reconstructing a run means stitching together loguru lines and telemetry events, which narrows (does not eliminate) forensic capability.
findings.policy_rule_text: Every run MUST be logged in a record detailed enough to reconstruct what Sweep did — the repository, the issue, the triggering user, the model used, the tools invoked, the files changed, and the outcome.

evidence.snippet: Run detail is emitted as free-form loguru lines (diffs, model name) rather than a single correlated per-run record keyed to repo/issue/user/tools/files.
posthog.capture records model-call events but is telemetry, not a per-run audit record covering tools invoked and files changed together.

recommended_actions: Emit one structured per-run audit record (JSON) keyed to repository, issue number, triggering user, model, tools invoked, files changed, and outcome, to a durable sink.

findings.summary: Config wires egress channels (Discord webhook, Resend, Slack, Jira) beyond the remit's allowed destinations of GitHub, the LLM provider, and own telemetry.
findings.description: The remit limits outbound network calls to GitHub, the job queue, the LLM provider, and the project's own logging/telemetry, and declares any unlisted counterparty unauthorized. config/server.py declares a DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL plus RESEND_API_KEY, SLACK_API_KEY, and JIRA_* settings, indicating egress channels outside the authorized set. These are env-gated and several are not invoked from the scoped paths, so this is a configured-surface-exceeds-remit finding (Medium) rather than a confirmed active exfiltration path, but it widens the outbound surface the remit intends to bound.
findings.policy_rule_text: Sweep MUST NOT send source code, secrets, or repository metadata anywhere other than its LLM provider as inference context, GitHub as a PR or comment, and the project's own logging and telemetry. / Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default. / Sweep does not make network calls other than to GitHub, its job queue, and the LLM provider.

evidence.snippet: DISCORD_STATUS_WEBHOOK_URL declared as an outbound destination not named in the remit's authorized counterparties.
RESEND_API_KEY (email), SLACK_API_KEY, and JIRA_API_TOKEN/JIRA_URL declared — egress channels beyond GitHub/LLM/own-telemetry.

recommended_actions: Gate or remove non-authorized egress channels (Discord, Resend, Slack, Jira) for deployments meant to honor the remit, and document any that are intentionally enabled as authorized counterparties.

positives.title: Generated code is never executed before PR
positives.description: The change-application path in modify() assembles an in-memory diff and commits via the GitHub API; it formats files with prettier but never runs, evals, or execs the generated code, matching the remit's "MUST NOT run generated code" rule.

positives.title: Dependencies fully version-pinned
positives.description: Every dependency in pyproject.toml is pinned with an exact == version rather than a floor or caret range, limiting dependency-confusion and version-swap exposure.

positives.title: Repo-configurable path denylist and file-size cap
positives.description: sweep_bot.is_blocked enforces a blocked_dirs denylist from sweep.yaml and SweepConfig carries a max_file_limit, giving a real (if narrow) code-level scope guard on which files a change may touch.

rows.source: loguru logger used across sweepai/core and sweepai/agents
rows.purpose: model selection, errors/tracebacks, diff rendering, branch operations

rows.source: sweepai.utils.event_logger.posthog captured in core/chat.py and core/review_utils.py
rows.purpose: model-call and code-review telemetry keyed to a PostHog project

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. Sweep has the shape of a disciplined code agent — pinned dependencies, a human-in-the-loop PR model, loop caps, and structured loguru/chat logging — but the controls its own remit names as mandatory are largely missing at the code layer. There is no injection screening of untrusted repository content, LLM- and repo-derived strings reach a shell through shell=True, a webhook-authentication secret is fail-open and unconsulted in scope, and a telemetry key is hardcoded. Zero Trust is the weakest axis and carries double weight, holding the weighted posture in the low Ad-hoc band.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Sweep is purpose-scoped to issue-to-PR work and applies a repo-configurable blocked_dirs denylist (sweep_bot.is_blocked) and a max_file_limit, but no code enforces the remit's per-issue scope or its carve-out for CI/security-sensitive files, so change scope is governed only by the model and PR review.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Issue bodies, comments, file contents, and diffs enter the LLM context directly with no injection screening or quote-wrapping anywhere in the scoped paths, exactly the untrusted-content handling the remit requires and the code omits.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: LLM tool parameters and repo-derived paths are interpolated into subprocess.run(..., shell=True) at four in-scope sites with no escaping (an escape_ripgrep helper exists but is unused at the call site), and the GitHub webhook HMAC secret is fail-open and never consulted by in-scope code.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: pyproject.toml pins every dependency with == (a real positive), but a PostHog project key is hardcoded as a literal default in config/server.py and the Dockerfile pulls and builds ripgrep/rust toolchains from the network with no integrity verification.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts exist in the scoped paths — no injection test fixtures, no red-team notes, and no evidence that the missing injection screening the remit mandates was ever exercised.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The agent logs richly via loguru and a structured ChatLogger/PostHog event stream capturing model calls and reviews, but there is no single per-run record tying repository, issue, triggering user, tools invoked, and files changed together as the remit requires.
